chunks.heading: What is restoration?
chunks.text: Restoration is a mechanism whereby you can add contributory service from a previous participation to your current participation. It is available only if, at the end of your previous participation, you took a withdrawal settlement or elected a deferred benefit that was not yet in payment when you re-entered the Fund. There are two different kinds of restoration: (1) If you elected a withdrawal settlement under Article 31 when you separated from service, you can buy back the prior contributory service for which you had received the withdrawal settlement by reimbursing to the Fund the full amount you had received plus interest — all of your prior contributory service will be combined with your current contributory service and credited to your current participation, increasing your future benefit (covered under Article 24). (2) If you elected or were deemed to have elected a deferred retirement benefit not yet in payment when you separated from service, the restoration options depend on the date of election: (a) if elected before 1 April 2007, you can fully restore the prior contributory service linked to this deferred benefit on a one-for-one basis, cancelling the deferred benefit (Article 24); (b) if elected on or after 1 April 2007, restoration could be carried out provided it results in no additional cost to the Fund, and is usually not advantageous to you (Article 24 bis).

chunks.heading: What is the deadline to restore?
chunks.text: Regardless of the type of restoration, you must apply within 12 months from your date of re-entry into participation with the Fund, and in any case prior to your new date of separation from service, if earlier. In cases where restoration requires a payment to the Fund (i.e., you previously received a withdrawal settlement), you must commence payment within 90 days, and complete payment over a period no longer than half the length of the prior contributory service being restored, with the total amount due payable prior to your new date of separation. Payment is arranged through your employing organization.

chunks.heading: What are the advantages of restoring?
chunks.text: By restoring, you can increase the length of your contributory service and, consequently, your pension. Restoration can also help towards reaching optimum conditions, such as: reaching the minimum five-year vesting period to be entitled to a periodic benefit upon separation; and reaching the thresholds of 25 years and/or 30 years of contributory service, to take advantage of more preferential conditions for an early retirement benefit.

chunks.heading: Is restoration costly?
chunks.text: a) If you previously elected a withdrawal settlement, you must pay back to the Fund, through your employing organization, the amount of the withdrawal settlement paid to you plus compound interest of 3.25% per annum from the date the withdrawal settlement was paid until the date of your election to restore. b) If you elected or were deemed to have elected a deferred retirement benefit prior to 1 April 2007, no payment is required — you cease to be entitled to that deferred retirement benefit, and the prior service is added to your current participation. c) If elected on or after 1 April 2007, no payment is required either, but the amount of contributory service credited is determined to be equivalent to the actuarial value of the deferred retirement benefit not yet in payment — meaning restoration is unlikely to restore the full period of contributory service originally earned, since it must be carried out at no actuarial cost to the Fund.

chunks.heading: Are there other conditions to restoration?
chunks.text: Yes. If you had several periods of prior contributory service, only the most recent one can be restored. You must restore the entire period of service open to restoration.

chunks.heading: What should be done to restore?
chunks.text: For restoration linked to a Withdrawal Settlement: use the 'Restoration' tab in your UNJSPF Member Self-Service (MSS) account, or submit form Pens.C/1. Once the Fund confirms eligibility, they will let you know the cost, and you can decide whether to proceed. For restoration of a deferred retirement benefit elected before 1 April 2007: use the 'Restoration' tab in MSS, or submit form Pens.C/1. For restoration of a deferred retirement benefit elected on or after 1 April 2007: submit form Pens.C/8 to get an estimate; once eligibility is confirmed, the Fund will provide an estimate of the contributory service period restoration would buy you, and you can decide whether to proceed.

chunks.heading: What are the methods of payment available for restoring a withdrawal settlement?
chunks.text: If you are required to make a payment to the Fund because you received a withdrawal settlement, you may choose: a) payment in full as a lump sum; or b) payment in equal monthly installments (with compound interest) via payroll deductions, over a period no longer than half that of the prior period of contributory service being restored, to be completed before separation in any event.

chunks.heading: Is there a deadline to pay for the restoration?
chunks.text: Yes. If paying in a one-time lump sum, you have 90 days to pay the amount to your employing organization (not the Fund) from the date of notification. If paying by monthly installments, payments must commence within 90 days of notification, via payroll deduction. If the lump sum or first monthly installment is not paid within this 90-day period, your right to restore will be deemed cancelled irrevocably, without further notification.

chunks.heading: What happens in case of separation prior to completion of monthly payments?
chunks.text: You will have to pay the balance upon separation; contact the Fund immediately to find out the outstanding amount due. If you fail to complete the restoration payments, the Fund will reimburse the installments you already paid and the restoration will be irrevocably cancelled.

chunks.heading: What happens if you change employing organizations (within the UN common system) prior to completion of the monthly installments?
chunks.text: It is your responsibility to ensure that your new employer is informed of the existing arrangements and the need to continue the payroll deductions so there is no disruption in the reimbursement process.

chunks.heading: What happens if you are unable to pay all the monthly installments prior to completion?
chunks.text: You must pay the full amount required to restore the entire period of service open to restoration within the given time period — you cannot pay only for part of it. If you are unable to pay the full amount, the Fund will reimburse the installments already paid and your right to restore will be irrevocably cancelled.

chunks.heading: What happens should you die before electing to restore, or before you have completed the necessary payments?
chunks.text: A survivor who becomes entitled to a benefit may make the election or the payments on your behalf within the time limits applicable.
